tools.name: Microsoft Foundry Agent Service
tools.description: Fully managed agent platform with deep Microsoft 365 integration and broad model access.
tools.pricing: Free platform access; pay for tokens and tools.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Best fit when agent delivery needs to stay close to Azure, M365, and enterprise identity controls rather than a standalone framework stack.

tools.name: AWS Bedrock Agents
tools.description: Modular AWS agent platform combining guided Bedrock Agents with framework-agnostic AgentCore deployment.
tools.pricing: Pay-per-use for Bedrock and AgentCore features.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Strong choice for AWS-centric teams that want managed agent primitives and infrastructure alignment without standardising on one open source framework.

tools.name: Google Agent Builder + ADK
tools.description: Open platform pairing the Agent Development Kit with managed agent services on Vertex AI.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Useful when Vertex AI, search, and Google-first app surfaces matter, but maturity and enterprise standardisation still need close validation.

tools.name: CrewAI
tools.description: Open source multi-agent framework focused on autonomous teams and task coordination.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Good for fast multi-agent prototypes, but larger regulated deployments usually need extra guardrails around observability, change control, and execution boundaries.

tools.name: AutoGen
tools.description: Multi-agent framework from Microsoft now in maintenance mode after Agent Framework consolidation.
tools.statusNote: Maintenance mode. Microsoft merged AutoGen with Semantic Kernel into the Microsoft Agent Framework.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Flexible for research-heavy agent patterns, though teams normally need to add stronger production discipline around orchestration and runtime governance.

tools.name: LlamaIndex
tools.description: Agent and retrieval framework with strong indexing, data connectors, and orchestration support.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Often a pragmatic fit when retrieval and data connectors matter as much as agent logic, especially for knowledge-heavy enterprise workflows.

tools.name: Semantic Kernel
tools.description: Microsoft orchestration SDK evolving into the unified Microsoft Agent Framework direction.
tools.statusNote: Evolving into the Microsoft Agent Framework.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Natural fit for Microsoft-leaning engineering teams that want code-first orchestration with .NET and enterprise integration paths.

tools.name: Microsoft Agent Framework
tools.description: Microsoft's new multi-language agent framework for building, orchestrating, and deploying agent workflows.
tools.statusNote: Microsoft positions Agent Framework as the migration target for Semantic Kernel and AutoGen users.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Worth watching for teams standardising on Microsoft agent patterns, but adoption should still be treated as early and architecture-led.

tools.name: LangGraph
tools.description: Stateful agent orchestration framework with graph-based execution and durable workflows.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Strong option when teams want explicit stateful control over agent flows instead of opaque black-box autonomy.

tools.name: Mastra
tools.description: Agent framework focused on composable workflows, tooling, and modern developer ergonomics.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.licenseWarning: Mastra uses a dual-license model: most of the repo is Apache 2.0, while code under ee/ directories uses the Mastra Enterprise License.
tools.practitionerNote: Promising TypeScript-first framework for product teams that want modern developer ergonomics, but enterprise proof points are still earlier than older stacks.

tools.name: OpenAI Agents SDK
tools.description: Production successor to Swarm for agentic OpenAI workflows with broader model support.
tools.statusNote: OpenAI Swarm was replaced by the Agents SDK.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Fast path for OpenAI-centric agent builds, though portability and provider concentration should be weighed before making it a platform default.

tools.name: Atomic Agents
tools.description: Minimalist agent framework emphasizing composability and predictable building blocks.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Best suited to teams that want minimal abstractions and tight control, not large batteries-included enterprise workflow tooling.

tools.name: BeeAI Framework
tools.description: Open agent framework formerly known as Bee Agent Framework, now under Linux Foundation governance.
tools.statusNote: Renamed from Bee Agent Framework; governance moved from IBM to Linux Foundation.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Interesting IBM-backed open framework for governed agent patterns, but most teams should still validate ecosystem depth before broad standardisation.

tools.name: Azure Logic Apps
tools.description: iPaaS workflow automation service with AI-enhanced orchestration and broad connector coverage.
tools.logoNotes: Official Microsoft Azure architecture/service icon from Azure_Public_Service_Icons_V23.
tools.practitionerNote: Best when the core problem is governed enterprise workflow automation with Azure connectors, not frontier agent behaviour or open-ended autonomy.

tools.name: AWS Step Functions
tools.description: Serverless state machine orchestration service for AWS-native workflows and automation.
tools.logoNotes: Official AWS architecture icon from Icon-package_01302026.
tools.practitionerNote: Strong fit for AWS-native production workflows that need explicit state, retries, and operational control rather than a higher-level AI builder UX.

tools.name: Google Cloud Workflows
tools.description: Serverless workflow orchestration for Google Cloud services and HTTP APIs.
tools.pricing: $0.01 per 1K internal steps; $0.025 per 1K external steps.
tools.logoNotes: Official Google Cloud workflow icon from the Google Cloud icon library.
tools.practitionerNote: Pragmatic choice for GCP-centric teams orchestrating APIs and services, but usually not the first pick for richer agent-builder experiences.

tools.name: n8n
tools.description: Workflow automation platform with strong self-hosting support and broad integration coverage.
tools.licenseWarning: n8n uses a Sustainable Use License, not MIT or Apache 2.0. It is more accurately described as source-available.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Often the fastest path for ops-heavy teams that want broad integrations and self-hosting, though complex enterprise controls may need extra discipline around change management.

tools.name: Paperclip
tools.description: Open source control plane for orchestrating persistent AI agent teams, budgets, and approvals.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Interesting control-plane option when budgets, approvals, and persistent agent operations matter, but still earlier than more established workflow stacks.

tools.name: LangFlow
tools.description: Visual flow builder for LLM pipelines and agent workflows with self-hosting support.
tools.statusNote: DataStax Cloud is shutting down April 9, 2026. Self-hosting remains available.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Useful for visual experimentation and internal workflow builds, though larger enterprise rollouts should validate long-term governance and platform direction after the IBM/DataStax shift.

tools.name: Dify
tools.description: Application development and workflow platform for LLM apps with plugin and enterprise features.
tools.licenseWarning: Dify's modified Apache 2.0 restricts multi-tenant deployment and requires retaining Dify branding without a commercial license.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Good fit for teams that want one self-hostable surface for apps, workflows, and model operations, but the license and commercial boundary need review before standardising widely.

tools.name: Flowise
tools.description: Low-code orchestration and agent workflow builder now under Workday ownership.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Practical low-code orchestration layer for teams moving quickly, provided they validate security posture and ownership-roadmap fit for production use.

tools.name: Haystack
tools.description: LLM pipeline and retrieval orchestration framework rebranded toward the Haystack Enterprise Platform story.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Strong option when retrieval pipelines and modular backend orchestration matter more than no-code UX or broad business-user automation.

tools.name: Rivet
tools.description: Workflow builder for AI pipelines with desktop-first self-hosting support.
tools.statusNote: No releases in 8+ months. Appears to be in maintenance or slow development.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Can still work for narrow visual pipeline use cases, but the maintenance signal makes it a cautious choice for new enterprise standardisation.

tools.name: Azure AI Content Safety
tools.description: Content moderation and safety service with configurable severity scoring and on-prem options.
tools.logoNotes: Official Microsoft Azure architecture/service icon from Azure_Public_Service_Icons_V23.
tools.practitionerNote: Useful when teams already run on Azure and need turnkey moderation and safety classifiers, but it is narrower than a full policy/governance control plane.

tools.name: AWS Bedrock Guardrails
tools.description: Configurable safety layer for filtering, PII handling, hallucination reduction, and policy controls.
tools.pricing: $0.15 per 1K text units after price cut.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Best fit for Bedrock-centric deployments that want managed prompt/content guardrails close to model invocation instead of a separate governance stack.

tools.name: Google Model Armor
tools.description: Model-agnostic prompt and response safety screening layer across Google enterprise infrastructure.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Worth evaluating for Google-first estates that need managed prompt and response protection, especially around injection and unsafe content controls.

tools.name: NeMo Guardrails
tools.description: Open source guardrails framework for LLM and agent safety with a dedicated Colang DSL.
tools.pricing: Free OSS plus NVIDIA NIM options.
tools.statusNote: Repository moved from NVIDIA/NeMo-Guardrails to NVIDIA-NeMo/Guardrails.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Good fit when teams want code-defined conversational and retrieval guardrails they can run themselves, accepting more engineering ownership than a managed service.

tools.name: Guardrails AI
tools.description: Validation-focused guardrails framework with a large validator ecosystem and freemium model.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Practical for Python-heavy application teams that want validator-style output controls in app code rather than buying a full enterprise governance platform.

tools.name: LLM Guard
tools.description: Open source safety filtering toolkit whose development has slowed following acquisition activity.
tools.statusNote: Development slowed significantly post acquisition. Last meaningful commit noted as December 2025 in the guide.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Best for security-minded teams that want open source scanning around prompts and outputs and are comfortable assembling their own policy pipeline.

tools.name: Rebuff
tools.description: Prompt injection defense project archived in May 2025 and not recommended for new projects.
tools.statusNote: Archived May 16, 2025. Do not recommend for new projects.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Most relevant as a lightweight prompt-injection defense layer in custom stacks, not as a broad end-to-end AI governance product.

tools.name: Lakera Guard
tools.description: Commercial LLM security layer integrating into broader cloud security offerings after acquisition.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Strong option when security and abuse prevention need a managed specialist layer that can sit in front of multiple model/application surfaces.

tools.name: Arthur GenAI Engine
tools.description: Agent governance and discovery platform following Arthur AI's January 2026 GenAI Engine rename.
tools.statusNote: Rebranded from Arthur AI to Arthur GenAI Engine and pivoted toward agent discovery and governance.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Better suited to enterprises that want governance tied to observability, evaluation, and runtime risk monitoring rather than isolated content filters alone.

tools.name: GitHub Copilot
tools.description: Coding assistant platform delivered through IDE plugins with individual, business, and enterprise tiers.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Safe default for enterprises standardising AI coding help broadly, especially when GitHub, VS Code, and policy controls already sit in the delivery path.

tools.name: Amazon Q Developer
tools.description: AWS coding assistant integrated into IDE workflows with agentic request limits and AWS-native context.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Best fit for AWS-heavy engineering teams that want coding help tied closely to cloud context, IAM, and internal AWS workflows.

tools.name: Gemini Code Assist
tools.description: Google coding assistant for IDE workflows with a generous free completion tier.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Worth considering for Google Cloud-centric teams, but enterprise rollout quality still depends heavily on how much of the stack already lives in Google tooling.

tools.name: Cursor
tools.description: Standalone AI coding IDE with rapid enterprise growth and credit-based pricing controversy.
tools.statusNote: Switched to credit-based pricing in June 2025, causing backlash.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Strong for fast-moving product teams that optimize for developer speed, though central governance and cost predictability need more scrutiny than incumbent platform copilots.

tools.name: Windsurf
tools.description: Standalone coding IDE shaped by major 2025 acquisition turbulence and later Cognition ownership.
tools.statusNote: OpenAI acquisition deal collapsed; Google hired key staff; Cognition acquired remaining product and brand.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Can be attractive for teams chasing agentic coding workflows, but ownership churn means larger enterprises should validate roadmap stability before standardizing.

tools.name: Microsoft 365 Copilot
tools.description: Productivity assistant tightly integrated with Microsoft 365, Graph, and enterprise workflow data.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Natural fit when the real value is inside Microsoft 365 workflows and Graph-connected knowledge, not just generic chat over documents.

tools.name: Gemini for Workspace
tools.description: Workspace assistant now bundled across plans with Google productivity and collaboration integration.
tools.statusNote: Bundled into Workspace plans in January 2025 with higher base pricing.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Pragmatic for Google Workspace estates that want AI features embedded in existing docs, mail, and meetings without a separate assistant rollout.

tools.name: Amazon Q Business
tools.description: Enterprise business assistant with many data connectors and the lowest entry pricing in this segment.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Useful when enterprises want a connector-heavy internal assistant with AWS alignment, especially if a lower entry price matters for broad rollout.

tools.name: Copilot Studio
tools.description: Low-code platform for building and publishing copilots and agents across Microsoft channels.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Best when business teams need to assemble Microsoft-native copilots quickly, provided architecture and environment sprawl are still governed centrally.

tools.name: Amazon Q Apps
tools.description: No-code AI app builder delivered as a feature within Amazon Q Business Pro.
tools.pricing: Included in Amazon Q Business Pro.
tools.statusNote: Feature within Q Business Pro, not a standalone product.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Most relevant for lightweight internal app generation inside an existing Amazon Q Business deployment, not as a standalone enterprise app platform.

tools.name: Gemini Enterprise
tools.description: Enterprise agentic AI platform and successor packaging for the former Agentspace product line.
tools.statusNote: Agentspace has been absorbed into Gemini Enterprise.
tools.logoNotes: Generated placeholder badge in repo, not a reviewed official product/project mark. Replace only after primary-source provenance is found.
tools.practitionerNote: Worth evaluating for Google-first organizations that want assistant and agent capabilities under one commercial umbrella, but packaging maturity should still be checked closely.
